draft.introduction: The Acharanga Sutra is the oldest surviving Jain text and the first of the eleven Angas. Its opening section articulates the principle of ahimsa (non-violence) with a thoroughness unmatched in any other religious tradition.

translation.section: Book 1, Lecture 1: Knowledge of the Weapon
translation.text: All beings are fond of life; they like pleasure, hate pain, shun destruction, and love to live long. To all, life is dear. The one who knows this and still causes harm to any living being—whether by action, by speech, or by thought, whether by commanding, consenting, or approving—injures his own soul. A wise person should not act sinfully toward earth, water, fire, air, or any being. This is the teaching of the path to liberation.

translator_notes.note: The Jain concept of ahimsa extends far beyond the prohibition of killing. It encompasses harm through speech and thought, and it applies to all categories of living beings, including elemental beings (earth-bodies, water-bodies, fire-bodies, air-bodies). This radical expansion of the moral community to include elements and microorganisms distinguishes Jain ethics from all other traditions.
